retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Salivary Gland Excision -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The most common indication for submandibular gland excision is salivary stones with secondary ductal obstruction and sialadenitis. Transoral stone removal may be successful in cases of an isolated calculus within the submandibular duct (see Image . Submandibular Gland Calculi). Sialendoscopy is a widely used minimally invasive treatment that can be both diagnostic and therapeutic, particularly when stones are smaller than 7 mm in diameter (see Image . Endoscopy View from Sialendoscopy). [15] However, most patients with salivary calculi experience recurrent obstruction and sialadenitis that do not resolve with minimally invasive measures alone, often necessitating submandibular gland excision.

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Salivary Gland Excision -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Salivary flow impairment, arising from factors such as dehydration, obstruction, and aging, can thicken saliva and promote stone formation. Salivary calculus composition varies by gland. Submandibular stones typically contain 70% to 80% inorganic material and 20% to 30% organic matter, with the latter including proteins (5%), lipids (1%), and carbohydrates. [6] [7] Reduced salivary flow also increases infection risk, frequently presenting in urgent and emergency care. Recurrent painful salivary stones, especially when complicated by bacterial sialadenitis, strongly warrant submandibular gland excision, which will be explored further in this activity.

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Salivary Gland Excision -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Preoperative medical optimization is essential since excision is usually performed under general anesthesia and frequently offered to older patients with recurrent calculi. Individuals who are poor candidates for general anesthesia may be better treated with conservative approaches. Conditions like trismus that prevent sufficient mouth opening for visualization and effective access contraindicate an intraoral approach to gland removal.

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Salivary Gland Excision -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The Wharton duct and its accompanying vein are then identified, with the vein ligated. Deeper dissection allows identification and protection of the hypoglossal nerve, which confirms the identity of the Wharton duct. The duct is ligated as close as possible to the sublingual papilla to prevent retention of calculi after resection.

retrieved_docs.title: Sialolithiasis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Management of sialolithiasis should begin with conservative measures, including massaging the salivary gland, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, and sialogogues. Signs of infection, including cervical adenopathy, purulent discharge from the salivary ducts, or erythema surrounding the salivary ducts, indicate the need for antibiotic therapy. [4] [11] Further treatment is dictated based on the sialolith's size, number, and location if conservative management is unsuccessful. Mobile submandibular stones measuring less than 5 mm located within the distal duct should initially undergo management with endoscopy. Impacted submandibular stones within the distal duct and stones larger than 5 mm should have treatment with transoral duct slitting. Stones of 5 to 7 mm within the proximal duct or hilar region should receive initial treatment endoscopically. If this is unsuccessful or the stone becomes impacted, the next step is a transoral surgical approach.

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Salivary Gland Excision -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Imaging is generally obtained before surgical excision. Ultrasound or contrast-enhanced computed tomography of the neck can characterize neoplastic processes and identify the presence, size, and location of calculi. [18] Cytological evaluation should precede surgical excision when a neoplasm is suspected. Fine needle aspiration or core needle biopsy, often performed under ultrasound guidance, are the primary methods. [19] Core needle biopsy yields a larger sample and reduces nondiagnostic results, but this procedure is associated with higher complication rates, such as bleeding. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Sialadenitis and Sialadenosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic sialadenitis: Medical management includes hydration, oral hygiene, pain relief, and sialogogues. In cases of infection, broad-spectrum antibiotics are added. In the case of sialolithiasis, salivary gland stone removal should occur using interventional sialendoscopy or direct surgical removal. [10] [11] Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy, under ultrasonic guidance, is used for intraglandular duct stone removal.Recurrent sialadenitis (>3 episodes/year) or in chronic sclerosing sialadenitis: excision of the salivary gland is the recommendation.

retrieved_docs.title: Sialolithiasis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: External shockwave lithotripsy (ESWL) is an option for not palpable stones or visualized under endoscopy. Intraparenchymal stones can be extracted endoscopically if they are 5 to 7 mm and visualized. Larger stones require treatment with transoral slitting. ESWL is indicated for smaller stones that are not palpable and not visualized endoscopically. ESWL is generally unsuccessful for stones larger than 7 to 10 mm. Surgical excision of the submandibular gland should be a last resort. [11] [20] [21] [22] Salivary stones within the parotid duct that measure less than 7 mm and are mobile require endoscopic removal. If endoscopic management is unsuccessful or the stones have become impacted, external shockwave lithotripsy is considered the most appropriate second-line therapy with subsequent endoscopic removal of fragmented stones. Treatment of salivary stones that do not respond to external shockwave lithotripsy is with a combined transcutaneous and endoscopic approach (assuming the stone is visible under endoscopy). Surgical excision of the parotid gland should be a last resort. [11] [21] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Salivary Gland Excision -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: With an annual incidence of approximately 60 cases per 100,000 individuals, salivary gland pathologies are frequently encountered by primary and emergency care providers, as well as otolaryngologists and head-and-neck surgeons. [30] Up to 1% of the population may develop salivary calculi, which may or may not be symptomatic. Acute suppurative sialadenitis, although comparatively rare (0.02% of hospital admissions), carries a mortality rate of up to 40%. [31] [32] Recognition of these conditions is therefore critical for most clinicians.

retrieved_docs.title: Parotitis -- Treatment / Management -- Obstructive Causes
retrieved_docs.content: Conservative management is the cornerstone of sialolithiasis. Clinicians should instruct patients to stay well-hydrated, massage the duct of the gland, apply warm compresses, discontinue anticholinergic medications if possible, and increase salivary flow by sucking on tart candies, such as lemon drops. Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are appropriate analgesics and help decrease inflammation, although patients with severe pain may require opioids. Patients with a suspected secondary infection should receive dicloxacillin or cephalexin. Patients who fail conservative management can undergo sialoendoscopy to facilitate stone removal using forceps and a wire basket. If a stone is larger than 4 mm, clinicians can combine sialoendoscopy with laser-assisted lithotripsy.

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Salivary Gland Excision -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Submandibular gland excision has no absolute contraindications, but several relative contraindications and considerations should be recognized before surgery. The presence of a malignant neoplasm often requires more than simple gland excision, as management may necessitate additional lymph node dissection, radiation therapy, or chemotherapy. Acute infection in or around the gland complicates dissection and removal, making preoperative treatment with antibiotics advisable before attempting surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Salivary Gland Excision -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Assess patients with submandibular gland disease for indications and contraindications to submandibular gland excision. Determine the most suitable approach to submandibular gland excision based on pathology, patient factors, and surgical considerations. Implement evidence-based, personalized strategies for managing common and rare complications of submandibular gland excision. Collaborate with all members of the interprofessional team, including primary and emergency care providers, oncologists, otolaryngologists, anesthesiologists, and nursing staff, to provide efficient, comprehensive, and coordinated care for individuals with submandibular gland disease. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Salivary Gland Excision -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Salivary Gland Excision -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Submandibular salivary gland excision provides definitive treatment for patients with recurrent or refractory disease, as well as for neoplasms and symptomatic ranulas. Compared with conservative modalities, excision eliminates the diseased gland and prevents recurrence, though at the cost of greater invasiveness. Indications include chronic sialadenitis, recurrent calculi, and selected tumors. Contraindications are limited to poor operative candidacy or unresectable malignancy. Potential complications include lingual nerve or marginal mandibular nerve injury, hematoma, infection, fistula, and scarring. Reported success rates exceed 90%, making excision a reliable option when conservative measures fail.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Sublingual Gland -- Clinical Significance -- Sialolithiasis
retrieved_docs.content: Salivary stones (calculi) obstructing an excretory duct is a common salivary gland disease. The pathophysiology of salivary calculi is related to salivary stasis and overall inflammation of the excretory duct. Salivary stones may cause swelling of the duct or gland causing colicky peri-prandial pain. Salivary calculi are manually palpable when lodged in a duct. Ultrasound can aid diagnosis along with adjunctive CT or MR sialography if workup is negative, but suspicion remains high. Initial treatment is conservative, which comprises oral hydration and sialagogues. Surgery is only recommended when a conservative approach fails, and symptoms persist. Sialolithiasis mainly impacts the submandibular gland and rarely occurs in the sublingual gland [3] .

retrieved_docs.title: Sialolithiasis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: MR sialography demonstrates similar sensitivity and specificity to NCCT for the evaluation of sialolithiasis. Benefits of MR include excellent visualization of the salivary ducts without the need for contrast and no radiation exposure. Disadvantages include cost, image acquisition time, and limited availability compared to NCCT and Ultrasonography. [3] [16] Sialendoscopy allows for direct visualization of salivary stones and the salivary ducts, thus providing excellent sensitivity and specificity. In addition to aiding in the diagnosis of sialolithiasis, clinicians increasingly use sialoendoscopy for therapy and stone removal, given the advancements in endoscopic technology. [3] Research has shown sialoendoscopy to be a safe and effective alternative to conventional open surgical techniques with a more favorable complication profile. [18] Additionally, sialoendoscopy is performable in the outpatient setting utilizing local anesthesia.

retrieved_docs.content: Excision is presented as a broad surgical category that encompasses a variety of operative methods across multiple organ systems. Specific procedures classified as methods of Excision include adrenalectomy (partial or complete) with or without adrenal gland biopsy performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches; arthroscopic removal of loose bodies from joints; cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration and choledochoenterostomy; choledochotomy or choledochostomy with ductal exploration, drainage, calculus removal, and optional transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty; exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region; nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct; and parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration, including variants that involve mediastinal exploration, sternal split, or transthoracic approaches. Additional method designations link Excision to therapeutic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, as

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1619
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is described as a broad surgical approach that encompasses a variety of operative techniques used to remove tissue, explore anatomical regions, or treat disease. Specific procedures classified under the Excision method include partial or complete adrenalectomy with or without adrenal gland biopsy performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal routes; cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, with or without choledochoenterostomy; exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial locations; suboccipital craniectomy aimed at cranial‑nerve exploration or decompression; radical orchiectomy for tumor removal that incorporates abdominal exploration; and parathyroidectomy procedures that involve mediastinal or cervical exploration, sometimes requiring a sternal split or transthoracic approach. In addition, Excision is linked to other therapeutic interventions on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, indicating that it

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 453
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is presented as a broad surgical category that encompasses a variety of operative techniques used for diagnostic, therapeutic, and exploratory purposes across multiple organ systems. Specific procedures classified under Excision include partial or complete adrenalectomy or adrenal gland exploration (performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally, with or without biopsy), arthroscopic removal of loose bodies from joints, cholecystectomy with common duct exploration accompanied by cholangiography or choledochoenterostomy, craniectomy or craniotomy for supratentorial exploration, suboccipital craniectomy for cranial nerve decompression, laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common duct exploration, radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration, and parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via a sternal split approach. In addition, Excision is linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, as well as to percutaneous nephrolithotomy

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1712
retrieved_docs.content: Excision serves as a broad parent category for a variety of surgical actions, including the set of procedures labeled “Clinical actions A‑G.” Within this overarching category, many specific operative methods are classified as forms of excision: cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (both open and laparoscopic), choledochotomy or choledochostomy with duct exploration, drainage or calculus removal (with or without cholecystotomy and with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty), transcranial orbital exploration with lesion removal, cervical and lumbar laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) that decompress nerve roots and may involve partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, or disc excision, radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration, and parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration performed via a cervical approach or a sternal split approach. At the same time, the excision category explicitly excludes several laminectomy techniques used for spinal stenosis: thoracic laminectomy with cord or cauda equina decompression without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for one or two vertebral segments; and cervical, lumbar, or thoracic laminectomy for more than two vertebral segments performed under the same constraints. Excision is also linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, indicating that excisional techniques extend beyond deep organ surgery to include facial and upper aerodigestive tract interventions. This integrated view highlights excision as a versatile surgical concept that encompasses numerous specific operative methods while delineating clear boundaries for procedures it does not cover.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1868
retrieved_docs.content: Lithotomy is a surgical category that encompasses the specific technique of removal of calculus of urethra with incision; this procedure is a particular form of lithotomy designed to address urethral stones (حصاة الإحليل). The removal of calculus of urethra with incision focuses directly on extracting calculi from the urethra, providing a targeted approach for managing urethral stone disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 183
retrieved_docs.content: maxillary antrostomy is specifically categorized as a diagnostic procedure on the nose, mouth, and pharynx. Examination of the urinary system includes incision and exploration of the urethra, a child procedure of the general “procedures on the urethra” group. Within the lithotomy family, removal of urethral calculus with incision (urethral stone extraction) is a subclass, illustrating the use of incision for stone removal. Prostatolithotomy and prostatotomy with calculus removal are placed under “other or therapeutic procedures; male genital,” with prostatolithotomy further containing child procedures such as شق البروستاتا عبر الإحليل (prostate incision through the urethra). Reopening of an osteotomy site of the facial bone is linked to “other or therapeutic procedures on the musculoskeletal system,” emphasizing its position within musculoskeletal therapeutic surgery. These hierarchical relationships support accurate coding, documentation, and comprehensive patient‑care planning by

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1737
retrieved_docs.content: Nephrolithotomy for removal of calculus (procedure) is a surgical intervention designed to extract kidney stones and it incorporates nephrotomy and nephrostomy as subordinate techniques. Nephrotomy refers to an incision into the renal parenchyma to directly access and remove the calculus, while nephrostomy involves creating a percutaneous tract for drainage or instrument passage. By including these child procedures, nephrolithotomy provides a comprehensive approach to stone removal and postoperative renal management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2167
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is a broad surgical category that falls under the parent group “Clinical actions A‑G.” It includes a wide range of operative methods such as partial or complete adrenalectomy (with or without biopsy) performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches; cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct, both with and without cholangiography; choledochotomy or choledochostomy with duct exploration, drainage, or calculus removal, optionally combined with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty; transcranial exploration of the orbit with lesion removal; laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and disc excision in a single lumbar interspace; laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration; radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration; and parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration performed via cervical or transthoracic approaches. The Excision category explicitly excludes certain laminectomy procedures: lumbar laminectomy with exploration and decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina that involves more than two vertebral segments, and cervical laminectomy with similar exploration that does not include facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy. In addition to these operative techniques, Excision is linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx and is related to plastic procedures on the nose. This integrated framework illustrates how Excision groups diverse anatomical surgeries while defining specific procedural exclusions and connections to related therapeutic domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 255
retrieved_docs.content: in head‑and‑neck (otolaryngologic) surgery, serving therapeutic procedures of the nose, mouth, and pharynx. It also constitutes the operative component of percutaneous nephrolithotomy, a minimally invasive urologic approach to kidney‑stone removal, and is integral to plastic procedures on the nose, encompassing both reconstructive and cosmetic nasal surgery, thereby linking excision to aesthetic surgical practice. The hierarchical organization—“Clinical actions A‑G” as the overarching parent, with Exploration and Excision as distinct but overlapping subcategories—provides clinicians a structured taxonomy for accurate procedural coding, documentation, and planning across endocrine, hepatobiliary, neurosurgical, ophthalmic, spinal, orthopedic, otolaryngologic, urologic, plastic, and oncologic specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 276
retrieved_docs.content: Nephrolithotomy for removal of calculus (procedure) is a surgical intervention specifically associated with the management of calculus of urinary tract, indicating its primary role in treating urinary stones. Within this overarching procedure, the techniques of nephrotomy and nephrostomy are considered child components, reflecting a hierarchical relationship in which these sub‑procedures are employed as part of the nephrolithotomy approach to access, fragment, and drain the urinary tract calculi. Understanding this procedural linkage aids clinicians in selecting appropriate operative strategies for effective stone removal and postoperative care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1728
retrieved_docs.content: Extracorporeal lithotripsy; urinary is a non‑invasive therapeutic technique used to address the pathology of حَصاة (urinary stones). By delivering focused shock waves to the stone, the procedure fragments the حَصاة into smaller pieces that can be passed spontaneously, thereby reducing the need for more invasive surgical interventions and improving patient outcomes in the management of urolithiasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 443
retrieved_docs.content: be used prophylactically to prevent stone formation. Recognizing the class memberships, adverse‑effect profiles, procedural roles and contraindications of these allowed qualifiers is essential for optimal patient care and for the accurate interpretation and application of laboratory and therapeutic processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2570
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is a broad surgical category that includes a wide range of operative techniques across several organ systems. It encompasses adrenalectomy procedures—both partial and complete, or exploration of the adrenal gland with or without biopsy performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, including cases with excision of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—as well as choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage, or calculus removal, which may be combined with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty. Excision also covers exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the supratentorial region, and various laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) techniques for cervical and lumbar nerve‑root decompression that may involve partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and excision of herniated intervertebral disc. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration is another method classified under Excision, along with multiple forms of parathyroidectomy—standard cervical parathyroidectomy, parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via cervical, sternal split, or transthoracic approaches, and parathyroidectomy combined with mediastinal exploration. Excision is further linked to “other or therapeutic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx” and is associated with plastic procedures on the nose, reflecting its inclusion in the broader grouping “Clinical actions A‑G.” However, certain spinal procedures are explicitly excluded from the Excision category, notably laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy (e.g., procedures for spinal stenosis involving one or two vertebral segments). This integrated overview delineates the diverse methods that fall under Excision, clarifies which procedures are excluded, and highlights its relevance for procedural classification and patient‑care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1037
retrieved_docs.content: the core operative step. Otolaryngologic and facial procedures include excision for exploration of the orbit via a transcranial route, for removal of lesions, and for therapeutic interventions on the nose, mouth, and pharynx. Excision is further described as the method for nipple exploration with removal of the lactiferous duct and for parathyroidectomy accompanied by mediastinal exploration, whether approached cervically or transthoracically. Beyond these specialties, excision is identified as the technique underlying percutaneous nephrolithotomy, a minimally invasive method for kidney stone removal, and is linked to various other therapeutic procedures on the nose. Although excision is widely applicable, it is explicitly excluded as the method for extensive thoracic laminectomy with exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina that spans more than two vertebral segments. In addition to its procedural role, excision is referenced as an answer to a specific
